name: Eye Screening for Children
snippet:
6 to 12 months · 12 to 36 months · 3 to 5 years · 5 years and older. Read more
The American Academy of Ophthalmology advises parents to seek a comprehensive eye exam if: their child fails a vision screening vision screening is inconclusive or cannot be done referred by a pediatrician or school nurse their child has a vision complaint or observed abnormal visual behavior, or is at risk for developing eye problems.

name: Vision Screening Guidelines by Age
snippet: Recommended practices for vision screening in pre-school-age children : A 2025 update. Schedule recommends vision screening for toddlers ages 1 and 2 years.

name: Childhood Eye Examination in Primary Care | AFP
snippet: 2023 · Cited by 6 — The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends beginning vision screening at four to five years of age and as early as three years of age in cooperative children ... Read more

name: Member Resources
snippet: Ages 48-59 months: Must correctly identify the majority of the optotypes on the 20/40 line to pass. Refer children who fail photoscreening. 5 years and older*. Read more

name: Recommended practices for vision screening in pre-school
snippet: 2025 · Cited by 7 — The NCCVEH recommends that children aged 3 to <6 years have their vision screened annually to identify potential eye health and vision disorders. These updated ... Read more

name: Children's Vision
snippet: This fact sheet provides an overview of the process and key components of a comprehensive eye exam for school-aged children

name: Policy Statement on Childhood Vision Screening
snippet: Jun 29, 2022 — Vision screenings should be available throughout childhood including birth to < 3 years, preschool, and school-aged years. Read more

name: Pediatric Eye Evaluations PPP 2022
snippet: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends vision screening at least once for all children aged 3 to 5 years to detect amblyopia or its risk ... Read more
